title: The Start of World War II
summary: World War II began in 1939 after Nazi Germany invaded Poland, following years of aggressive expansion and failed appeasement by Britain and France. The invasion triggered declarations of war and turned a European crisis into a global conflict.

concepts.title: Europe After World War I
concepts.summary: The peace settlement of 1919 created a Germany that deeply resented its terms but retained the capacity to challenge them. The Great Depression destroyed the fragile democratic stability of the 1920s and created the conditions in which Adolf Hitler could rise to power.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The peace settlement that ended the First World War was negotiated at Paris in 1919 by the victorious Allied powers without the participation of the defeated nations. The Treaty of Versailles, signed with Germany in June 1919, was the most consequential and most controversial. Germany lost approximately thirteen percent of its prewar territory and ten percent of its population, including Alsace and Lorraine returned to France, the Rhineland placed under Allied occupation and permanently demilitarized, and large portions of eastern territory ceded to the newly created Polish state. Germany's army was limited to one hundred thousand professional soldiers, its navy stripped of its capital ships, and it was forbidden to maintain an air force. Under Article 231 — the war guilt clause — Germany was required to accept responsibility for causing the war, the legal foundation for the reparations that followed.

The principal architects of the settlement were Georges Clemenceau of France, David Lloyd George of Britain, and Woodrow Wilson of the United States, and their conflicting aims produced a settlement that satisfied none of them fully. Wilson's League of Nations — designed as a collective security organization that would make future wars preventable through international cooperation — was fatally undermined from the beginning by the refusal of the United States Senate to ratify the treaty or join the League. The most powerful economy in the world withdrew from the international commitments Wilson had made on its behalf and returned to non-entanglement in European affairs.

sections.title: Key players
body: Germany experienced hyperinflation in 1923 that wiped out the savings of the middle class and left permanent psychological scars on a generation. The Great Depression that began in 1929 devastated the global economy but hit Germany with particular severity, producing mass unemployment — by 1932, over six million Germans were officially unemployed, roughly a third of the workforce — and destroying the fragile confidence in the Weimar Republic's democratic institutions that the relative prosperity of the mid-1920s had begun to build.

The Weimar Republic — the democratic government established in Germany after the war and the Kaiser's abdication — was born under inauspicious circumstances. It had been founded in the chaos of military defeat, signed an armistice that nationalist politicians immediately characterized as a betrayal — the stab-in-the-back myth that blamed Germany's defeat on Jewish and socialist subversion of the home front — and been forced to accept the Versailles terms that the German public widely regarded as unjust. It faced violent challenges from both left and right and never commanded the wholehearted loyalty of the military, judiciary, or civil service.

sections.title: What happened
body: The instability of the interwar period was not confined to Germany. Across central and eastern Europe, the new states created by the peace settlement were ethnically mixed, economically weak, and politically fragile. Italy, a nominal victor in the war but one that felt cheated of promised territorial rewards, had turned to fascism under Mussolini by 1922. The Soviet Union, emerging from revolution and civil war, was committed in principle to the overthrow of the capitalist order. The fundamental problem with the Versailles settlement was not simply that it was too harsh or too lenient but that it was too harsh to be accepted by Germany as legitimate while being too lenient to permanently disable Germany's capacity to overturn it.

A Germany that had accepted the settlement as just would have lived within it. A Germany that had been genuinely disabled — militarily occupied, economically reduced to a level that made rearmament impossible — would have been unable to overturn it. What actually existed was a Germany that deeply resented the settlement, retained the industrial and demographic capacity to challenge it, and lacked the political leadership that might have found a way to address its grievances within the existing international framework.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The Weimar Republic was born under inauspicious circumstances and never fully escaped them. It had signed an armistice that nationalists immediately characterized as betrayal, been forced to accept Versailles terms the German public regarded as unjust, and faced violent challenges from left and right. When the Depression destroyed the economic conditions that had allowed it to function with some degree of stability in the late 1920s, it had few reserves of popular legitimacy to draw on.

The Great Depression was the turning point that transformed the instabilities of the 1920s into the catastrophe of the 1930s. By destroying economic prosperity across the industrial world, it discredited the liberal democratic governments that presided over it, drove desperate electorates toward radical alternatives, and created the conditions in which a man like Adolf Hitler could be not merely a fringe extremist but a serious contender for power.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The Great Depression destroyed whatever stability the Weimar Republic had built during the mid-1920s. As unemployment surged past six million and the government's response — austerity, deflation, rule by emergency decree — proved both painful and ineffective, voters abandoned the mainstream parties in enormous numbers. The Nazi Party received 2.6 percent of the vote in 1928, 18 percent in September 1930, and 37 percent in July 1932. Without the Depression, Nazism would almost certainly have remained a violent but marginal movement in German politics.

sections.title: Legacy
body: This concept is the essential background to everything that follows. Hitler's rise, German rearmament, the policy of appeasement, the Nazi-Soviet Pact, and the invasion of Poland all take place against the backdrop of a Europe destabilized by the First World War and its aftermath, that lacked the institutional and political capacity to manage the resulting tensions, and that was presided over by great powers whose commitment to maintaining the peace settlement was eroded by war-weariness, economic crisis, and the memory of the costs they had paid the last time they fought a major European war.

concepts.title: Hitler and the Nazi Takeover
concepts.summary: Adolf Hitler rose from obscurity to become chancellor of Germany in January 1933 and dictator of a one-party Nazi state by August 1934. Built on racial antisemitism, social Darwinism, and the concept of Lebensraum, Nazi ideology made expansion and racial conquest not just permissible but obligatory.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: Adolf Hitler was born in Austria in 1889, spent his young adulthood as a marginal figure in Vienna absorbing the virulently antisemitic and pan-German nationalist politics of the Austrian capital, served in the German army during the First World War, and experienced Germany's defeat in November 1918 as a personal and civilizational catastrophe that confirmed everything he believed about the betrayal of Germany by Jews, Marxists, and other internal enemies. After the war, he settled in Munich, joined the tiny German Workers' Party, quickly became its dominant figure, and transformed it into the National Socialist German Workers' Party.

The failed Beer Hall Putsch of November 1923 — an attempt to launch a national revolution by seizing power in Munich — sent Hitler to prison for nine months, during which he dictated Mein Kampf, an autobiographical manifesto that stated his political ideas with unusual frankness: his belief in the racial hierarchy of peoples, the necessity of acquiring living space in the east at the expense of the Slavic peoples, and the centrality of the Jewish question to everything wrong with Germany and the world.

sections.title: Key players
body: The Nazi ideology Hitler articulated was built on three interconnected foundations. The first was racial antisemitism: the belief that the Jews were a malevolent racial force working to destroy Germany through Marxism, financial exploitation, and the mixing of blood. The second was social Darwinism applied to nations: the belief that history was a struggle between races for survival and dominance, that this struggle was natural and inevitable, and that Germany's destiny was to prevail in it. The third was Lebensraum — living space: the belief that Germany's future required the conquest of vast territories in eastern Europe and Russia, to be cleared of their existing populations and settled by Germanic peoples.

The Nazi Party's electoral performance through the 1920s was modest — only 2.6 percent of the vote in 1928. The Depression changed everything. As unemployment rose and the Weimar government's response proved both painful and ineffective, voters abandoned the mainstream parties in enormous numbers. The Nazis received 37 percent of the vote in July 1932 — the largest share any single party had ever received in a Weimar election.

sections.title: What happened
body: The path from electoral success to dictatorial power ran through the machinations of conservative elites who believed they could use Hitler for their own purposes. Franz von Papen persuaded the aging President Hindenburg that appointing Hitler as chancellor with conservatives holding the key cabinet positions would give the Nazis' popular support without giving them real power. Hindenburg agreed in January 1933. The calculation proved catastrophically wrong.

Hitler used the powers of the chancellorship, the Enabling Act passed in March 1933 that gave him essentially unlimited legislative authority, the burning of the Reichstag (used to justify emergency decrees), and the systematic terror of the SA and SS to destroy the opposition within months. By the summer of 1933, all political parties except the Nazis had been banned or dissolved. When Hindenburg died in August 1934 and Hitler merged the offices of president and chancellor, the transformation of Germany into a one-party totalitarian state was complete.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The speed and completeness of the Nazi seizure of power still startles. A country with one of the most sophisticated intellectual, scientific, and cultural traditions in the world became a one-party dictatorship within eighteen months of Hitler's appointment as chancellor. The legal system was quickly subordinated to Nazi ideology. The civil service, judiciary, and military were purged of Jews and political opponents. Independent trade unions were abolished. The press was brought under state control. The Jews of Germany began to be systematically excluded from economic and professional life through legislation.

The domestic transformation of Germany was itself the precondition for everything that followed: without a totalitarian state capable of mobilizing German resources for war, without an ideology that made expansion and racial conquest not just permissible but obligatory, Hitler's foreign policy ambitions would have remained fantasy rather than becoming the catastrophe they proved to be.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The Night of the Long Knives in June 1934 — in which Hitler ordered the murder of the SA leadership and several other political figures — was the moment at which the regime crossed from political authoritarianism to the direct use of murder as an instrument of domestic politics. It also secured the loyalty of the professional military by eliminating the SA's ambition to replace the regular army. After the Night of the Long Knives, the army swore a personal oath of loyalty to Hitler rather than to the German state — a decision whose consequences would prove fateful.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The Nazi takeover is the necessary foundation for understanding everything that follows. German rearmament, the policy of appeasement, the specific character of the war Germany eventually launched — all were shaped by the nature of the regime that had taken power in Berlin. A different German government — even an authoritarian nationalist one — would not have pursued the same combination of ideological radicalism, racial obsession, and willingness to risk everything on the gamble of a new European war.

concepts.title: Appeasement
concepts.summary: Appeasement — the policy of making concessions to Nazi Germany's territorial demands in the hope of avoiding war — was pursued by intelligent statesmen with coherent reasons for their choices. The Munich Agreement of September 1938 was its culmination and its effective end, destroyed six months later when Germany occupied the rest of Czechoslovakia.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The policy of appeasement was not a policy of cowardice or ignorance, though it has often been characterized as both. It was pursued by experienced and intelligent statesmen who had coherent reasons for the choices they made. The British and French governments that practiced appeasement most consistently were operating in the shadow of the First World War in ways that it is difficult to fully appreciate from a later perspective. The war had killed nearly a million British and one and a half million French soldiers, and had demonstrated that modern industrial warfare was capable of producing casualties on a scale no one had anticipated. The determination not to repeat that experience was not a failure of nerve. It was a rational response to knowledge of what war actually cost.

Neville Chamberlain, who became British prime minister in May 1937, came to foreign affairs with strong convictions: that the grievances underlying German nationalism were to some degree legitimate, that a negotiated accommodation with Hitler was both possible and necessary, and that the most important thing was to avoid the catastrophe of another major European war. His famous claim on returning from Munich that he had secured 'peace for our time' is remembered as one of the most embarrassing statements in British political history, but it should be understood as the expression of a genuine hope rather than a cynical performance.

sections.title: Key players
body: The French government, under a succession of premiers in the 1930s, pursued a similar policy but from an even more defensive strategic posture. France had suffered proportionally greater losses than Britain in the First World War and was acutely conscious of its demographic and industrial disadvantage relative to Germany. French strategy in the interwar period was essentially defensive — exemplified by the Maginot Line, the elaborate system of fortifications along the Franco-German border — and the willingness to take offensive action against Germany was correspondingly limited.

The logic of appeasement rested on several assessments, some defensible and some wrong. The defensible assessments included the recognition that Britain and France were not militarily prepared for war against Germany in the mid-1930s, and that time spent negotiating might be used for rearmament. The wrong assessments included the belief that Hitler's aims were limited and would be satisfied by specific territorial concessions, and the belief that the threat of war would eventually produce a negotiated accommodation rather than accelerating Hitler's willingness to fight.

sections.title: What happened
body: The Munich Agreement of September 1938 was the culmination and the effective end of appeasement as a viable policy. Hitler had been demanding the Sudetenland — the German-speaking borderlands of Czechoslovakia — on the grounds of self-determination. Chamberlain flew to Germany three times in September to negotiate, eventually agreeing, with France's concurrence, that Czechoslovakia should cede the Sudetenland to Germany. Czechoslovakia, which had a well-equipped army and strong defensive fortifications in the Sudetenland, was not consulted. The Soviet Union, which had a mutual defense treaty with Czechoslovakia, was not invited to participate. In exchange for the Sudetenland, Hitler signed a declaration that the Munich Agreement represented his last territorial demand in Europe.

Six months later, in March 1939, German troops occupied the rump of Czechoslovakia. There was no German population to liberate, no self-determination argument to make. Hitler had simply taken a sovereign country whose independence Britain and France had effectively guaranteed at Munich. The occupation of Prague destroyed whatever credibility appeasement retained and made clear that Hitler's aims extended to the domination of Europe.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The failure of appeasement demonstrated the fundamental incompatibility between rational bargaining strategies and totalitarian aggression driven by ideological imperatives. Hitler was not a normal statesman pursuing limited, defined interests within a framework of international law and mutual obligation. He was pursuing a program of racial empire that required war. Concessions that would have satisfied a normal revisionist power merely lowered his estimate of the resistance he would face and accelerated his timeline.

Appeasement also damaged the credibility of British and French commitments to other European states. Poland, which received British and French guarantees after the occupation of Prague, could not be confident that those guarantees meant more than the implicit guarantee to Czechoslovakia had meant.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The occupation of Prague was the turning point that ended the appeasement era. After March 1939, the British government extended guarantees to Poland, Romania, and Greece and began serious negotiations with the Soviet Union about a possible alliance against Germany. The negotiations with the Soviet Union failed, partly because of mutual distrust and partly because of the intervention of a German diplomatic initiative that produced the Nazi-Soviet Pact. Appeasement's failure became the template for a subsequent Western strategic doctrine that the lesson of Munich — you cannot satisfy an aggressor through concessions — was universal and permanent.

sections.title: Legacy
body: Appeasement remains one of the most studied and most debated episodes in modern diplomatic history, partly because it failed so visibly and partly because its failure has been used to justify every subsequent policy of firmness toward authoritarian aggressors. Understanding what appeasement actually was, why it was pursued, and why it failed requires resisting the temptation to treat it as simply the product of weakness or naivety. It was a coherent policy that failed because the adversary it was applied to was not the kind of adversary that coherent diplomacy could have managed.

concepts.title: Rearmament & Expansion
concepts.summary: From 1933 to 1939, Hitler pursued a consistent pattern of calculated escalation — each move testing the resolve of Britain and France, each successful test emboldening the next. Rearmament, the remilitarization of the Rhineland, the absorption of Austria, and the dismemberment of Czechoslovakia were all accomplished without firing a shot in anger.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: Hitler's foreign policy in the years between his assumption of power in January 1933 and the invasion of Poland in September 1939 followed a consistent pattern of calculated escalation: each move tested the resolve of Britain and France, each successful test emboldened the next, and each concession by the Western powers confirmed Hitler's conviction that they would not fight until it was too late to stop him. German rearmament began almost immediately after Hitler came to power, initially in secret and then, after 1935, openly and defiantly. The reintroduction of conscription in March 1935 produced protests from Britain, France, and Italy but no concrete action.

The remilitarization of the Rhineland in March 1936 — the reoccupation of German territory that the Versailles Treaty had permanently demilitarized — was a far more significant gamble, because the German forces involved were small enough that French military action could almost certainly have forced them to withdraw. Hitler himself later admitted that if France had responded militarily he would have had to retreat. France did not respond, partly because of British reluctance to act and partly because French generals overestimated German strength. After the Rhineland, Hitler drew the obvious conclusion: the Western powers would not fight.

sections.title: Key players
body: The absorption of Austria — the Anschluss of March 1938 — was accomplished with remarkable ease. Austrian Nazis, encouraged and supported by Berlin, had been agitating for union with Germany, and the Austrian chancellor Kurt Schuschnigg, attempting to forestall a German takeover by calling a plebiscite on Austrian independence, was bullied into resigning by Hitler. German troops crossed the Austrian border on March 12th, 1938, and were greeted by large and genuinely enthusiastic crowds in Vienna. Austria ceased to exist as an independent state. Britain and France protested. Nothing happened.

Germany's defense spending increased from approximately 1 percent of national income in 1933 to over 20 percent by 1938. The Wehrmacht grew from the 100,000-man army permitted by Versailles to over three million men by 1939. The Luftwaffe, forbidden by Versailles and developed in secret during the early 1930s, became the most powerful air force in Europe. The tank forces were developed and organized by commanders like Heinz Guderian who had thought deeply about how to use armored forces in combination with motorized infantry and air support to achieve rapid breakthroughs — the Blitzkrieg concept that would devastate Poland and France.

sections.title: What happened
body: Within six years of coming to power, Hitler had remilitarized the Rhineland, reintroduced conscription, absorbed Austria, and dismembered Czechoslovakia — all without firing a shot in anger. The pattern then repeated with Poland. After the occupation of Prague in March 1939, Hitler turned his attention to the Polish Corridor and the city of Danzig, using the same combination of diplomatic pressure, nationalist agitation, and manufactured grievance that had worked with Austria and Czechoslovakia.

Germany's economic situation complicated the rearmament program in ways that shaped Hitler's strategic timeline. The massive military spending produced economic strains — inflation pressures, raw material shortages, balance of payments difficulties — that made a prolonged arms race unsustainable without either accepting economic decline or seizing the resources of neighboring countries. The economic case for early war was one of the factors that pushed Hitler toward action in 1939 rather than waiting for Germany's military buildup to reach its full potential.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The sequence of successful aggressive moves from 1935 to 1939 mattered for reasons beyond the specific territorial gains it produced. Each successful escalation confirmed Hitler's belief in his own infallibility — his conviction that his judgment was superior to that of his generals and his diplomats, who repeatedly counseled caution and were repeatedly proved wrong by the success of his gambles. This conviction of infallibility would eventually lead him to make strategic decisions — the invasion of the Soviet Union, the declaration of war on the United States — that were catastrophic for Germany.

The sequence also mattered for the internal politics of the Wehrmacht. Hitler had overcome the skepticism of his generals about the Rhineland, about Austria, about Czechoslovakia — and in each case the generals who had predicted disaster had been wrong. By 1939, the professional military's capacity to constrain Hitler's strategic decision-making had been significantly reduced, and the men who might have pushed back most effectively had been removed or marginalized.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The occupation of Prague in March 1939 was the turning point that ended the era of bloodless revisionism and made another general European war almost inevitable. After Prague, Hitler could no longer credibly claim to be a revisionist statesman correcting the injustices of Versailles. His next target was Poland, and the British and French governments had extended guarantees to Poland that, if they meant anything at all, committed them to fight. The question was whether those guarantees would prove more durable than the commitments to Czechoslovakia had proved to be.

sections.title: Legacy
body: German rearmament and expansion set the immediate military and strategic context for the invasion of Poland and the outbreak of war. It also created the asymmetry in military preparedness that would shape the early years of the war: Germany, having rearmed rapidly and specifically for offensive operations, had significant advantages over France and Britain, which had rearmed more slowly, more defensively, and in ways less well suited to the kind of war Germany intended to fight.

concepts.title: The Nazi–Soviet Pact
concepts.summary: The announcement on August 23, 1939 that Germany and the Soviet Union had signed a non-aggression pact shocked the world and removed the last realistic obstacle to German military action against Poland. The pact included a secret protocol dividing eastern Europe into German and Soviet spheres of influence.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The announcement on August 23rd, 1939 that Germany and the Soviet Union had signed a non-aggression pact came as a genuine shock to most of the world — including many members of the Communist Party who had spent years being told that fascism was the mortal enemy of communism and that the Soviet Union was the bulwark of anti-fascist resistance. The pact between Hitler and Stalin, negotiated in secret and announced with maximum dramatic effect just days before the German invasion of Poland, upended the assumptions of European diplomacy at the worst possible moment.

The path to the pact ran through the failure of Anglo-Soviet-French negotiations for a collective security arrangement against Germany. After the occupation of Prague in March 1939, the British and French governments had recognized that some arrangement with the Soviet Union was necessary if Germany was to be deterred from further aggression. Negotiations were opened, but they proceeded slowly and without urgency. The fundamental obstacle was Poland: the Soviet Union demanded the right to send troops through Polish territory in the event of a German attack, and Poland, with vivid historical memories of Russian occupation, refused to allow Soviet forces on its soil under any circumstances.

sections.title: Key players
body: Stalin's strategic calculus was ruthlessly self-interested. The Soviet Union was not ready for war with Germany — the purges of 1937 to 1938 had decimated the Red Army's officer corps, and Soviet military capacity was significantly weaker than it would be two years later. A pact with Germany would buy time for rearmament, eliminate the immediate threat of a German attack, and — through the secret protocol that accompanied the published non-aggression pact — deliver substantial territorial gains: eastern Poland, the Baltic states, Finland, and Bessarabia were all assigned to the Soviet sphere of influence.

Hitler's motivation was more straightforward. The German military's war planning depended on avoiding the nightmare of a two-front war. If Germany invaded Poland and Britain and France declared war, Germany would be at war in the west. Without a pact with the Soviet Union, it might simultaneously face Soviet intervention in the east — recreating the two-front scenario that had contributed to Germany's defeat in the First World War. The pact eliminated that threat, at least for the short term.

sections.title: What happened
body: The pact was negotiated with remarkable speed once both sides had decided they wanted it. German Foreign Minister Joachim von Ribbentrop flew to Moscow on August 22nd and signed the agreement the following day. The published text was a standard non-aggression pact, promising neutrality if either party was attacked by a third power. The secret protocol — not publicly acknowledged by the Soviet government until 1989 — divided eastern Europe into German and Soviet spheres of influence in a manner that anticipated the territorial changes that would follow in the coming months.

The ideological implications were profound and immediately disorienting. The Soviet Union and Nazi Germany had been presenting themselves to the world as ideological opposites: communism versus fascism. The pact required both sides to suppress or rationalize their ideological commitments in the service of immediate strategic interests. For communist parties and their sympathizers around the world, it was a genuine crisis of faith.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The immediate military consequence of the pact was that Germany could invade Poland without fear of Soviet intervention. The broader strategic consequence was that it demonstrated how completely the system of collective security envisioned by Versailles and the League of Nations had collapsed. There was no coalition of democratic powers capable of presenting a unified front against German aggression. Germany faced Britain and France alone — and Germany, rearmed and battle-tested, had significant military advantages over both.

The pact also had consequences that extended well beyond 1939. The Soviet occupation of eastern Poland in September 1939, of the Baltic states in 1940, and the subsequent invasion of Finland resulted in mass deportations, killings, and the destruction of civil society in the occupied territories — most infamously the massacre of approximately 22,000 Polish officers and other leaders at Katyn and other sites, carried out by the Soviet NKVD in the spring of 1940.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The pact was the diplomatic turning point that made the Second World War inevitable. Without it, Hitler might have hesitated to invade Poland, calculating that the risk of a two-front war was too great. With it, he had the security he needed to act. The pact also illustrates one of the most important lessons of the interwar period: that diplomatic arrangements treating authoritarian great power interests as compatible with the democratic order are liable to catastrophic failure when the authoritarian power decides that its interests are better served by a different arrangement.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The Nazi-Soviet Pact connects the failure of appeasement and collective security to the specific military event that triggered the war. It removed the last realistic obstacle to German aggression and ensured that Poland — and the world — would face the consequences of the failure to build a credible deterrent coalition in the years when it might still have been possible.

concepts.title: Invasion of Poland
concepts.summary: Germany invaded Poland on September 1, 1939, demonstrating the effectiveness of combined arms warfare — Blitzkrieg — that would dominate the early years of the war. The campaign lasted five weeks and incorporated from the beginning the ideological character that distinguished Nazi warfare from conventional military conflict.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The German invasion of Poland on September 1st, 1939 began at 4:45 in the morning, with German forces crossing the Polish frontier at multiple points simultaneously while the Luftwaffe struck Polish airfields, railway junctions, and military installations across the country. The invasion was accompanied by a propaganda pretext — a staged attack by SS troops in Polish uniforms on a German radio station at Gleiwitz — but no serious observer was deceived. What began on September 1st was a war of conquest, planned and prepared for months, whose objective was the annihilation of the Polish state and the incorporation of its territory into a German racial empire.

The campaign against Poland lasted five weeks and demonstrated the effectiveness of the German military concept that would dominate the early years of the war. German armored and motorized forces, supported closely by the Luftwaffe, penetrated the Polish lines at multiple points simultaneously, moving too fast for the Polish command to react effectively, cutting communications, encircling Polish formations before they could retreat. The Polish army, fighting with courage but without the armored and air forces to match the German attack, was overwhelmed. Warsaw fell on September 27th.

sections.title: Key players
body: The Polish military situation was made hopeless by the Soviet intervention on September 17th, which attacked Poland from the east under the terms of the secret protocol. Caught between two invasions simultaneously, Poland had no hope of successful resistance regardless of the quality of its army. The Polish government and military command escaped through Romania to Britain, where a Polish government-in-exile was established in London and Polish forces continued to fight alongside the Western Allies for the remainder of the war.

Behind the advancing front-line units came the Einsatzgruppen — mobile killing squads — whose mission was to execute Polish intellectuals, political leaders, clergy, and Jews, decapitating the social and intellectual leadership of the conquered country. Tens of thousands of Poles were killed in the first weeks of the occupation in a deliberate policy of cultural and social destruction. The campaign was not simply a military operation. From the beginning it incorporated the racial and ideological dimensions that distinguished Nazi warfare from conventional military conflict.

sections.title: What happened
body: The fall of Poland inaugurated the most brutal occupation regime in occupied Europe before the invasion of the Soviet Union two years later. Poland was divided between Germany and the Soviet Union, with the German-occupied territory itself divided: the westernmost parts were annexed directly into the Reich and their Polish populations expelled or reduced to a laboring underclass, while the central area was organized as the General Government under the brutal administration of Hans Frank.

Over the following years, the General Government became the site of the Holocaust — the industrial killing of the Jewish population of occupied Europe — with the extermination camps of Auschwitz-Birkenau, Treblinka, Sobibor, Belzec, Majdanek, and Chelmno all located in this territory. The invasion of Poland was thus not merely the beginning of the war but the beginning of the systematic genocide that would be the war's most distinctive and most horrifying feature.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The campaign's military lessons were quickly absorbed by both sides. The speed and effectiveness of German armored and air operations confirmed what German theorists had been arguing: that modern mobile warfare, properly executed, could produce results in weeks that positional warfare had been unable to achieve in years. The lesson that France and Britain drew — that they needed more and better tanks, more and better aircraft, more integrated operational concepts — was correct but drawn too slowly. When Germany turned west in May 1940, the Western Allies were not yet ready to meet it on equal terms.

The invasion also triggered the British and French declarations of war that are the formal beginning of the Second World War as a general European conflict. But those declarations were not accompanied by any immediate military action — the period that followed, known as the Phoney War, involved little fighting on the Western Front as both sides prepared for the larger conflict they knew was coming.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The invasion of Poland and the two-week Soviet intervention that followed destroyed the independent Polish state that had existed since 1918. For the peoples of Poland — caught between Nazi racial imperialism and Soviet communist totalitarianism, subjected to occupation, deportation, mass murder, and the eventual Holocaust — the invasion was the beginning of the most catastrophic six years in their country's modern history. Of Poland's prewar population of thirty-five million, approximately six million — roughly one in six — died during the Second World War, a proportion exceeded only by the Soviet Union.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The invasion of Poland connects the diplomatic background of the Nazi-Soviet Pact to the British and French declarations of war and the beginning of the general European conflict. It also establishes the character of Nazi warfare and occupation policy — the integration of ideological objectives into military operations — which will shape the entire subsequent history of the war.

concepts.title: Britain and France Declare War
concepts.summary: Britain and France declared war on Germany on September 3, 1939 after Germany failed to respond to their ultimatums demanding withdrawal from Poland. The declarations were followed by eight months of the Phoney War — little significant fighting on the Western Front as both sides prepared for the larger conflict they knew was coming.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: Britain and France declared war on Germany on September 3rd, 1939 — Britain at 11:00 in the morning, France at 5:00 in the afternoon — after Germany failed to respond to their ultimatums demanding the withdrawal of German forces from Poland. The declarations transformed the German-Polish conflict into the Second World War, committing two of the world's major powers to a conflict whose outcome no one could confidently predict and whose costs no one could accurately foresee.

The British declaration was announced to the nation by Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain in a radio broadcast that became one of the indelible memories of the war. Chamberlain's voice, weary and without triumph, conveyed the sense of a man whose hopes had been definitively destroyed: 'Everything I have worked for, everything I have hoped for, everything I have believed in during my public life, has crashed into ruins.' It was a statement of personal and political failure, and it was honest. The war was, in a very direct sense, the product of the failure of the policy he had pursued.

sections.title: Key players
body: Chamberlain's position was increasingly untenable after the declaration of war, and he would be replaced as prime minister in May 1940 by Winston Churchill, who had been the most consistent and most prominent critic of appeasement throughout the 1930s. Churchill's warnings about the threat posed by Nazi Germany — delivered in a succession of Commons speeches that had earned him political isolation and the reputation of an alarmist — were now retrospectively vindicated, and his energy, his rhetoric, and his capacity to articulate the moral stakes of the conflict in language that sustained popular will would prove essential to Britain's survival in the dark years of 1940 and 1941.

The French government faced its own severe internal tensions. A significant portion of French conservative and business opinion was more afraid of communism than of Nazism, and some French politicians regarded a German-dominated Europe as preferable to the alternative of another war that might destroy France as badly as the last one had. These attitudes — which would eventually produce the collaborationist Vichy regime after the fall of France in 1940 — were already visible in the half-heartedness with which some French officials approached the declarations of war.

sections.title: What happened
body: The declarations of war were followed, for eight months, by almost nothing on the Western Front. The period from September 1939 to May 1940 — the Phoney War — saw little significant fighting. British and French forces were deployed along the Belgian border and the Maginot Line, training and equipping and waiting. Germany was occupied with digesting Poland, transferring forces from east to west, and preparing the campaigns that would follow.

The Phoney War was not entirely inactive. At sea, the naval war began immediately — the British ocean liner Athenia was sunk by a German submarine on the first day of the war — and the naval struggle for Atlantic supply lines was already underway. In Scandinavia, Germany's invasion of Denmark and Norway in April 1940 ended the Phoney War illusion and exposed the inadequacy of Allied response: a British and French counter-landing in Norway was poorly organized and quickly defeated, contributing to the parliamentary crisis that brought Churchill to power in London.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The declarations of war mattered for what they committed Britain and France to, but also for what they did not do. They did not immediately rescue Poland, which was beyond rescue regardless of Allied intentions. They did not trigger any offensive action against Germany — the French army that could have pushed into the lightly defended Saar region in September 1939 did not do so. What they did was commit two major powers to a war whose eventual outcome — largely determined by events that neither could yet foresee, including Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union and Japan's attack on the United States — would prove fatal to the Nazi regime.

The declarations also committed Britain and France, and eventually their empires, to the enormous human and material costs of a second major war within a generation. For Britain, the Second World War would prove less costly in absolute military terms than the First, but the economic exhaustion and imperial overextension produced by six years of total war would contribute to the dissolution of the British Empire and Britain's displacement from the center of world power in the decades that followed.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The fall of the Chamberlain government and its replacement by Churchill's coalition in May 1940 was the political turning point that determined the character of British participation in the war. Churchill's refusal to consider a negotiated peace with Germany — a course that several members of the War Cabinet were willing to explore in the desperate days after Dunkirk — was the decision that made the eventual Allied victory possible. Without Britain's continued resistance, the United States would have had no foothold in Europe from which to eventually enter the conflict.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The declarations of war complete the diplomatic sequence that constitutes the formal beginning of the Second World War. They connect the invasion of Poland to the global conflict the war eventually became, and they establish the basic alignment of powers — Germany and its allies against Britain, France, and eventually the Soviet Union and the United States — that would determine the war's outcome.

concepts.title: The Phoney War
concepts.summary: The Phoney War — from September 1939 to May 1940 — was a period of formal war but little actual fighting on the Western Front. The choices made during it, especially Germany's adoption of the Manstein Plan, determined the catastrophic military outcome of May and June 1940 and the fall of France.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The Phoney War — the period from September 1939 to May 1940 when Britain and France were formally at war with Germany but when little significant fighting took place on the Western Front — is one of the stranger episodes in the history of the Second World War. After the frenzy of the crisis summer, the terror of the Polish invasion, and the solemnity of the declarations of war, Europe found itself in a prolonged, uneasy suspension. The armies faced each other across the Rhine and the fortifications of the Maginot Line. Soldiers dug trenches and trained and waited. But the great offensive that everyone expected did not come.

The Allied strategy during the Phoney War was essentially passive, waiting for Germany to act. The Maginot Line — a vast system of underground fortifications, artillery emplacements, and troop positions along the Franco-German border — was the physical embodiment of this defensive philosophy. The problem was that it did not extend along the Franco-Belgian border, because Belgium had declared neutrality and the Allies were reluctant to violate it by extending their defensive line through Belgian territory.

sections.title: Key players
body: The German operational planning during the Phoney War produced one of the most consequential strategic improvisations of the war. The original German plan for the western offensive — Fall Gelb, Case Yellow — was a relatively conservative variant of the old Schlieffen concept. But in January 1940, a German aircraft carrying plans for the operation made an emergency landing in Belgium, and copies apparently fell into Allied hands. The German high command was forced to reconsider.

General Erich von Manstein, whose operational proposal for a dramatically different approach had previously been rejected, now found a more receptive audience. Manstein's plan concentrated the main German armored thrust through the Ardennes forest — terrain the Allies considered unsuitable for large armored formations and had therefore left relatively lightly defended — and aimed at cutting the Allied forces in Belgium off from the French rear. The adoption of the Manstein Plan was the crucial decision of the Phoney War period, though neither side knew it at the time.

sections.title: What happened
body: The Phoney War ended abruptly on May 10th, 1940, when Germany launched simultaneous invasions of the Netherlands, Belgium, and Luxembourg, drawing the Allied forces forward into Belgium as planned, while the armored formations of Army Group A drove through the Ardennes and reached the Channel coast within ten days. The speed and audacity of the operation exceeded even the expectations of the German commanders who planned it. The Allied forces in Belgium, cut off from the French rear, were driven back to the coast at Dunkirk, where a remarkable naval evacuation rescued approximately 338,000 British and French soldiers, leaving behind all their heavy equipment.

France, its armies in disarray and its political will shattered, signed an armistice on June 22nd, 1940. The Wehrmacht had accomplished in six weeks what four years of fighting had failed to achieve in the First World War. The French army was not significantly inferior to the German army in numbers or equipment — it had more tanks than Germany and better armor in many categories. What it lacked was the operational concept, the command culture, and the organizational flexibility to respond effectively to the kind of mobile warfare Germany was fighting.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The Phoney War mattered because the choices made during it — by the German planners who adopted Manstein's audacious scheme, by the Allied commanders who deployed too confidently into Belgium, by the French politicians who had never developed the institutional capacity to fight total war — determined the catastrophic military outcome of May and June 1940. The fall of France left Britain alone, facing a Germany that controlled the entire coastline from Norway to the Spanish border, with no obvious path to victory.

The evacuation of Dunkirk and the fall of France also produced the political crisis that brought Churchill to power and that framed the entire subsequent British experience of the war: the miracle of the evacuation sustaining national morale, the trauma of the collapse feeding a determination never to be caught so unprepared again, and the isolation of Britain after France's armistice creating both the danger and the opportunity that the Battle of Britain would resolve.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The German offensive of May to June 1940 was the military turning point that transformed the war. If France had not fallen, if the Battle of France had produced the prolonged attritional struggle that the First World War had been, the subsequent history of the war would have been utterly different. The fall of France determined the shape of the war for the next two years and created the conditions — Britain's survival, American assistance, eventual Soviet and American belligerence — that would ultimately produce Germany's defeat.

sections.title: Legacy
body: The Phoney War connects the declarations of war to the dramatic military events of 1940. It raises one of the central counterfactual questions of the war: what might have happened if the original German plan had been used, or if the Allies had responded more effectively, or if France's political and military institutions had been more capable of sustained resistance? The answer is unknowable, but the question illuminates the degree to which the outcome of the Second World War was shaped by specific choices made by specific people at specific moments.

concepts.title: Why World War II Began
concepts.summary: The Second World War began because a totalitarian state with an expansionist, racially imperialist ideology achieved power in Europe's most populous and industrially capable country, and pursued its aims through a sequence of aggressive actions that the democratic powers were unwilling or unable to resist until the moment resistance became unavoidable.

sections.title: Setting the scene
body: The Second World War began because a totalitarian state with an expansionist, racially imperialist ideology achieved power in Europe's most populous and industrially capable country, pursued its aims through a sequence of aggressive actions that the democratic powers were unwilling or unable to resist until the moment resistance became unavoidable, and then launched a war of conquest that in its ideology and its methods was different in kind from the conventional great-power conflicts that had preceded it.

This explanation is simultaneously simpler and more complex than explanations historians have offered for the First World War. Simpler, because there is a more clearly identifiable cause: Adolf Hitler and the Nazi movement. More complex, because identifying Hitler as the cause immediately raises further questions. How did Hitler come to power? Why did the democratic powers fail to stop him earlier? What was the relationship between the specific ideology of Nazism and the broader conditions — the legacy of Versailles, the Great Depression, the failures of liberal democracy — that made his rise possible?

sections.title: Key players
body: Hitler was necessary but not sufficient. He needed Germany — its industrial capacity, its military tradition, its reserves of resentment about Versailles and defeat. He needed the Great Depression — to destroy the economic conditions that had given the Weimar Republic its only period of relative stability. He needed the failures of the Western powers — their appeasement, their inability to construct effective collective security arrangements, their unwillingness to pay the costs of early confrontation that might have prevented a later one. And he needed the Nazi movement itself — the party apparatus, the propaganda machine, the network of true believers who implemented his program at every level.

The structural conditions that made the Second World War possible were therefore multiple and interconnected. The peace settlement of 1919 created a resentful Germany without disabling it. The League of Nations created the form of collective security without the substance. The Great Depression destroyed the economic foundations of democratic stability. The appeasement policy gave Hitler the time and the territorial gains he needed to make war on his own terms. The failure of the Anglo-Soviet negotiations left him free to secure his eastern flank. Each of these conditions was necessary; none was sufficient by itself.

sections.title: What happened
body: The war that resulted from this confluence of conditions was different from what anyone had expected or planned. In its ultimate scope and character, it was a global conflict that involved the total mobilization of national economies, the deliberate targeting of civilian populations, the systematic murder of racial and ethnic minorities on a scale that had no precedent in modern history, and the development and use of weapons — including eventually nuclear weapons — that transformed the relationship between warfare and civilization.

The Holocaust — the systematic murder of approximately six million Jews and millions of others including Roma, people with disabilities, Soviet prisoners of war, and political opponents — was the most extreme expression of the ideological character that distinguished Nazi warfare from conventional military conflict. It was not a by-product of the war or a consequence of its pressures; it was an objective of the regime that had launched the war, pursued with bureaucratic organization and deliberate intent alongside and sometimes at the expense of the military campaign.

sections.title: Why it mattered
body: The Second World War produced consequences of the first magnitude for the world order that emerged from it. The defeat of Nazi Germany and the exposure of the Holocaust's horrors shaped the postwar human rights framework — the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the Genocide Convention, the Nuremberg principles — that represented an attempt to establish that certain actions by states were not merely regrettable but criminal. The war's outcome determined the shape of European politics for decades: the division of Germany, the Soviet occupation of eastern Europe, the Cold War that structured international relations for the next four decades.

The human costs were staggering. Total deaths from the Second World War are estimated at between seventy and eighty-five million, of which roughly thirty-five to forty million were civilians. The Soviet Union alone lost approximately twenty-seven million people. Poland lost roughly a fifth of its prewar population. The scale of destruction — physical, demographic, institutional — was without precedent in human history.

sections.title: Turning point
body: The turning point that ultimately determined the war's outcome was not in 1939 but in 1941: Germany's invasion of the Soviet Union in June and Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor in December brought into the war the two powers whose resources would eventually overwhelm Germany's. But these events were themselves the consequence of the war's outbreak in 1939 and of the specific way that outbreak unfolded — the fall of France leaving Hitler with a misleading sense of invincibility, the survival of Britain providing the platform from which the Allied counteroffensive would eventually be launched, and the character of Nazi ideology making the attack on the Soviet Union not merely a strategic option but an ideological imperative.

sections.title: Legacy
body: This final concept draws together the threads of the topic. Europe after Versailles created the structural conditions for instability. The Nazi takeover brought to power a regime with the will and eventually the capacity to exploit those conditions for an imperialist war. Appeasement failed to contain German expansion. Rearmament gave Germany military advantages that would take years to overcome. The Nazi-Soviet Pact cleared the last diplomatic obstacle to German aggression. The invasion of Poland triggered the declarations of war. The Phoney War set the stage for the military catastrophe of 1940. The war that began in September 1939 was not inevitable in the sense of having been determined by forces beyond human control — at every stage, different choices could have produced different outcomes. But it was the product of a convergence of forces, choices, and failures that, in combination, made it the most destructive conflict in human history.
